Keywords: sensitivity, tornado chart, assumption testing, variable impact, what-if, robustness, key drivers
Description: Systematically varies each input assumption to determine which ones most affect the decision outcome. Reveals decision drivers and noise.
When to Use: When the decision depends on uncertain assumptions and you need to know which ones actually matter
Inputs Required: Key variables, plausible range for each variable, a decision model or scoring framework
How to Apply: 1. Identify key input variables. 2. Define plausible range (low-base-high) for each. 3. Vary one variable at a time while holding others at base. 4. Record impact on decision outcome. 5. Rank variables by impact magnitude. 6. Focus resources on high-impact uncertain variables.
Output Format: Tornado chart showing which variables have the most impact on the decision; sensitivity table with breakpoints
Strengths: Reveals what matters most; prevents over-analysis of non-sensitive inputs; builds decision confidence
Limitations: Assumes variables are independent; one-at-a-time variation misses interaction effects; requires quantifiable model

Keywords: break-even, threshold, payback, minimum viable, go no-go, investment threshold, return
Description: Calculates the point at which an option becomes viable — the minimum performance needed to justify the investment.
When to Use: Evaluating go/no-go investment decisions or comparing options with different cost structures
Inputs Required: Fixed costs, variable costs per unit, revenue or value per unit, time horizon
How to Apply: 1. Identify all fixed costs of the option. 2. Identify variable costs per unit of activity. 3. Identify value generated per unit. 4. Calculate: break-even = fixed costs / (value per unit - variable cost per unit). 5. Assess plausibility of reaching break-even. 6. Calculate margin of safety.
Output Format: Break-even point (units or time); margin of safety; payback period chart
Strengths: Simple and clear; communicates viability threshold; helps set minimum success criteria
Limitations: Assumes linear relationships; may oversimplify complex cost structures; ignores qualitative factors

Keywords: decision tree, sequential choices, probability branches, expected value, optimal path, nodes, payoff
Description: Maps sequential decision points and chance events as a tree, assigns probabilities and payoffs to branches, calculates optimal path.
When to Use: When the decision involves sequential choices with probabilistic outcomes at each stage
Inputs Required: Decision points, possible outcomes at each point, probability estimates, payoff values for terminal nodes
How to Apply: 1. Draw decision nodes (squares) and chance nodes (circles). 2. Add branches for each option/outcome. 3. Assign probabilities to chance branches (must sum to 1.0). 4. Assign payoff values to terminal nodes. 5. Calculate expected value by rolling back from terminal nodes. 6. Choose the path with highest expected value.
Output Format: Decision tree diagram; expected value for each path; recommended action at each node
Strengths: Handles sequential decisions; makes probabilities and payoffs explicit; visual and communicable; handles option value
Limitations: Quickly becomes complex with many branches; requires probability estimates; assumes rational decision-maker

Keywords: scenario, futures, external uncertainty, environment, robustness, what-if world, plausible futures
Description: Evaluates how each option performs under multiple plausible future scenarios, testing robustness rather than optimizing for one forecast.
When to Use: When multiple external uncertainties interact and the future environment is genuinely unknowable
Inputs Required: 2-3 key external uncertainties, 2-3 plausible values for each, options to evaluate
How to Apply: 1. Identify 2-3 key external uncertainties. 2. Build scenario matrix from combinations. 3. Name and describe each scenario. 4. Evaluate each option under each scenario. 5. Identify which options are robust (perform well across scenarios). 6. Develop contingency plans for adverse scenarios.
Output Format: Scenario matrix table; option performance across scenarios; robustness ranking
Strengths: Handles deep uncertainty; avoids overcommitment to single forecast; builds preparedness
Limitations: Subjective scenario selection; does not assign probabilities; can generate too many combinations

Keywords: relative scoring, comparison, criteria-based, normalized, weighted, multi-criteria, ranking
Description: Scores options against criteria on a standardized scale, applies weights, and produces a transparent ranking.
When to Use: Comparing 3+ options across incommensurable criteria where a transparent ranking is needed
Inputs Required: Options list, evaluation criteria, weight per criterion, scoring rubric (1-5 scale)
How to Apply: 1. Define criteria and weights (sum to 100). 2. Create scoring rubric (what does 1-5 mean for each criterion). 3. Score each option independently on each criterion. 4. Calculate weighted score per option. 5. Rank by total weighted score. 6. Perform sensitivity check on weights.
Output Format: Scoring matrix with weighted totals; ranked option list; sensitivity notes
Strengths: Transparent and repeatable; handles diverse criteria; facilitates group alignment; auditable
Limitations: Precision can be illusory; weight selection is subjective; scores depend on rubric quality

Keywords: opportunity cost, forgone alternatives, true cost, what else, trade-off, next best alternative, sacrifice
Description: Evaluates the true cost of choosing an option by explicitly measuring what is sacrificed — the value of the next-best alternative.
When to Use: When choosing one option means forgoing others that have significant value
Inputs Required: All viable options, value estimates for each, constraints that force trade-offs
How to Apply: 1. List all viable options. 2. Estimate the value/benefit of each. 3. Identify which options become unavailable if you choose each one. 4. Calculate opportunity cost = value of best forgone alternative. 5. True cost = direct cost + opportunity cost. 6. Compare options on total cost basis.
Output Format: Opportunity cost table showing direct cost, forgone value, and total cost for each option
Strengths: Reveals hidden costs; prevents narrow framing; forces consideration of alternatives; improves resource allocation
Limitations: Value of alternatives may be hard to estimate; can create decision fatigue; not all forgone options are clear

Keywords: risk-reward, probability of success, impact, 2x2 matrix, risk profile, upside downside, positioning
Description: Positions each option on a 2x2 matrix of probability of success (x-axis) vs. magnitude of reward (y-axis). Reveals risk-adjusted option positioning.
When to Use: When options have distinctly different risk profiles and you need a visual comparison
Inputs Required: Each option's estimated probability of success and magnitude of reward/impact, plus downside risk
How to Apply: 1. List all options. 2. Estimate probability of success for each (0-100%). 3. Estimate magnitude of reward if successful. 4. Plot on 2x2 matrix. 5. Categorize: high-prob/high-reward (do first), high-prob/low-reward (easy wins), low-prob/high-reward (strategic bets), low-prob/low-reward (avoid). 6. Select based on risk appetite.
Output Format: 2x2 matrix with options positioned; quadrant labels; recommended priority order
Strengths: Visual and intuitive; enables portfolio thinking; quick to create; good for communication
Limitations: Two-dimensional simplification; probability estimates may be unreliable; ignores correlation between options

Keywords: bayesian, prior probability, posterior, evidence update, likelihood, new information, belief revision
Description: Formally updates probability estimates when new evidence arrives during the decision process, preventing both over-reaction and under-reaction.
When to Use: When new evidence arrives during the decision process and you need to update beliefs rationally
Inputs Required: Prior probability estimate for each option, new evidence, likelihood of evidence under each hypothesis
How to Apply: 1. State prior probabilities for each option being correct. 2. New evidence arrives. 3. Ask: How likely is this evidence if Option A is correct? If Option B? 4. Apply Bayes rule: Posterior = (Likelihood x Prior) / Evidence. 5. Update probabilities. 6. Check if new probabilities change the decision.
Output Format: Updated probability table; decision shift analysis; confidence change log
Strengths: Rational evidence integration; prevents overreaction to single data points; cumulative learning; tracks belief evolution
Limitations: Requires probability estimates; computationally involved for many options; prior selection is subjective

Keywords: pre-mortem, prospective hindsight, failure analysis, risk identification, blind spots, assume failure, what could go wrong
Description: Assumes the decision has already been made and failed. Participants work backward to identify what went wrong, surfacing risks invisible in forward-looking analysis.
When to Use: Before committing to a high-stakes or irreversible decision where hidden risks could be catastrophic
Inputs Required: Proposed decision, team of 3-12 people, 30-60 minutes
How to Apply: 1. State the proposed decision. 2. Fast-forward: imagine it is 12 months later and the decision failed spectacularly. 3. Each person independently writes what went wrong. 4. Share all failure modes. 5. Categorize by likelihood and severity. 6. Develop mitigation plans for high-risk items.
Output Format: Ranked list of failure modes with likelihood, severity, and mitigation plans
Strengths: Surfaces hidden risks; overcomes optimism bias; psychologically safe; works well in groups; simple to facilitate
Limitations: Can be demoralizing if overused; may generate unlikely risks; depends on honest participation

Keywords: reference class, base rate, outside view, historical comparison, similar decisions, prediction accuracy, anchoring to data
Description: Predicts outcomes by comparing the current decision to a reference class of similar past decisions, using actual outcomes rather than inside-view estimates.
When to Use: When estimating costs, timelines, or outcomes of a chosen option where similar past decisions exist
Inputs Required: Identification of similar past decisions, their actual outcomes, adjustments for material differences
How to Apply: 1. Identify reference class (similar past decisions). 2. Gather actual outcome data (cost, time, success rate). 3. Calculate distribution (mean, range). 4. Identify where current decision sits relative to class. 5. Adjust for material differences. 6. Use reference class prediction as baseline, not inside-view estimate.
Output Format: Base rate prediction; confidence interval from reference class; comparison to inside-view estimate; adjustment rationale
Strengths: Counters planning fallacy; grounded in real data; simple to explain; highly accurate in aggregate
Limitations: Requires finding truly comparable decisions; reference class selection is subjective; unique situations may lack reference class
